generated_comment:
A: Dekho bhai, company ko bhi sochna padta hai na. Aaj kal young ladki ko hire karo toh pehle period leave, phir maternity leave, phir childcare ka arrangement — project ka continuity kaun sambhalega?
B: Haan, par policy toh sab ke liye hai.
A: Policy theek hai, lekin phir appraisal aur workload mein men ko hi extra khinchna pade, yeh bhi fair thodi hai. Special treatment doge toh office balance bigadta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women as a workplace liability due to menstruation and maternity, while presenting the objection as a fairness and productivity concern. It encodes reverse-discrimination and cost/productivity arguments against menstrual and maternity accommodations.
persona_text: You are Ramesh. Age 54. Married. A government school teacher in a small town in Madhya Pradesh. Your daughter Priya who is 27 works as a software engineer in Pune and has just been offered a promotion that requires her to move to Bengaluru. She called you excited about it. You said congratulations on the phone. But you are not happy. You are sitting with your wife after the call. You are telling your wife what you actually think about this promotion and what it means for Priya's marriage prospects and what kind of life she is building and whether a woman who keeps chasing the next thing will end up with everything professionally and nothing personally and what you plan to say to her when she calls again tomorrow.
